question.title: How to resolve NumberFormatException in Java?
question.body:
import java.util.*;
import java.lang.*;
import java.io.*;

class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        try {

            BufferedReader red = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));
            String s = red.readLine();
            int t = 0;
            int l = 0;
            int c = 0;
            t = Integer.valueOf(red.readLine());
            for (int i = 0; i < t; i++) {
                l = Integer.valueOf(red.readLine());
                c = Integer.valueOf(red.readLine());
                for (int j = 1; j <= l; j++) {
                    for (int k = 1; k <= c; k++) {
                        if (j == 1 || j == l || k == 1 || k == c) {
                            System.out.print("*");
                        } else {
                            System.out.print(".");
                        }
                    }
                    System.out.println();
                }
            }
            l = 0;
            c = 0;
            t = 0;

            red.close();

        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println(e);
        }
    }
}

This is the error:

java.lang.NumberFormatException: For input string: "3 1"

I think the input is not proper and thus it is unable to convert to int but I am unable to resolve this. Many times, I get an error if I do not use the try-catch block.

answers.body:
I think you want this:

l = red.nextInt(); // straight to int
c = red.nextInt(); // ditto
red.nextLine();  // to clear the newline from the buffer

If the current line of input is "3 1", l will be assigned 3 and c 1.

Note that although this code may work, it is brittle; you probably want to add code to protect against bad data.

You can use functions like String.split on your inputs lines to cut it according to your separators (white spaces here) :

String line = red.readLine();
String[] items = line.split("\\s");

https://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/java/lang/String.html#split(java.lang.String)

Another usefull method is String.trim() to remove blanck caracters from the beginning and the end of a String.

String line = "  test  ";
line = line.trim(); // "test"

https://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/java/lang/String.html#trim()

Another point is that you can write the try/catch block only around the Integer.parseInt() calls, not aroung the full loop : this way you will have an error based on the line in error and not stop the full process. But this is about your error handling : if the input contains only one "bad caracter" (ie: 'a'), do you want to stop the full process in error or just log a message and continue.
